questions.question: Explain the concept of minimum distance in block codes. How does it determine error detection and correction capability?
keyPoints: Minimum distance (d_min) is the smallest Hamming distance between any two valid codewords
For error detection: can detect up to (d_min - 1) errors
For error correction: can correct up to floor((d_min-1)/2) errors
Example with d_min=5: detects 4 errors, corrects 2 errors

questions.question: What is a Hamming code? Explain how it achieves single-bit error correction with an example of (7,4) Hamming code.
keyPoints: Hamming code is a linear block code for single-bit error correction
Parity bits placed at positions 1,2,4 (powers of 2)
Each parity bit checks specific bit positions using binary representation
For (7,4): 4 data bits + 3 parity bits, relation: 2^3 >= 7+1 = 8
Syndrome indicates error position directly

questions.question: Differentiate between error detection and error correction codes. Give examples of each.
keyPoints: Error detection: identifies if error occurred, requests retransmission
Error correction: identifies location and corrects errors without retransmission
Examples of detection: CRC, Parity check, Checksum
Examples of correction: Hamming code, Reed-Solomon
Correction codes need more redundancy than detection codes

questions.question: Explain the working of CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Check) for error detection.
keyPoints: CRC uses polynomial division concept
Sender divides data by generator polynomial G(x)
Remainder (CRC bits) is appended to data
Receiver divides received data by same G(x)
Non-zero remainder indicates error
Can detect burst errors up to length of generator polynomial degree

questions.question: What is syndrome decoding in block codes? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Syndrome is calculated as S = H × r^T where H is parity-check matrix
If S = 0, no error (or undetectable)
If S ≠ 0, syndrome indicates error pattern
For single-error-correcting codes, each non-zero syndrome corresponds to specific bit error
Example: In (7,4) Hamming, syndrome 101 might indicate error at position 5

questions.question: Explain 2D Parity Check with a suitable example. How does it achieve single-bit error correction?
keyPoints: Data is arranged in rows and columns
Parity bit added to each row and each column
For single-bit error, the error row and error column can be identified
Intersection of error row and column gives error location
Error bit is flipped to correct it
Can detect even number of errors but cannot locate them

questions.question: Define code rate and explain its significance in block coding. How does it relate to error protection?
keyPoints: Code rate = k/n where k is data bits, n is total transmitted bits
Represents efficiency of code in transmitting information
Higher code rate = more data, less redundancy
Lower code rate = more redundancy, better error protection
Trade-off between efficiency and reliability
Example: (7,4) Hamming has rate 4/7 ≈ 0.57

questions.question: Explain the classification of block codes. Discuss linear block codes, cyclic codes, and provide differences with examples.
keyPoints: Block codes: fixed-size data blocks with redundancy added
Linear block codes: form vector space, closure under addition (examples: Hamming, Parity check)
Cyclic codes: cyclic shift property (examples: CRC, BCH)
Non-linear codes: no linear structure
Systematic vs non-systematic forms
Error correction capability depends on minimum distance
Applications: data storage, communication, digital television

questions.question: Explain Single Parity Check (SPC) and 2D Parity Check. Compare their error detection and correction capabilities with suitable examples.
keyPoints: SPC: adds one parity bit (even/odd parity)
SPC detects odd number of errors only, no correction
2D Parity: matrix format with row and column parity
2D Parity detects all odd errors, can correct single-bit errors
2D Parity detects double errors in same row or column but not all double errors
Example SPC: data 1010 with even parity = 10101
Example 2D: 4x4 data with row and column parity bits
Trade-offs: SPC simple but limited, 2D better correction but more overhead

questions.question: Describe the Hamming code in detail. For a (7,4) Hamming code: (a) Show the codeword structure (b) Encode data 1101 (c) Explain error correction with an example error pattern.
keyPoints: Hamming code for single-bit error correction
Parity bits at positions 1,2,4; data bits at 3,5,6,7
Parity calculation using binary position representation
For data 1101: parity bits calculated to form complete codeword
Syndrome calculation: S = H × r^T
Non-zero syndrome gives error position
Error correction: flip the bit at error position
Example with error: received vector with one bit flipped, syndrome points to error location

questions.question: Explain the concept of syndrome decoding in block codes. How is it used for error detection and correction? Describe the decoding process.
keyPoints: Received vector r = c + e where c is codeword, e is error vector
Syndrome S = H × r^T = H × e^T
If S = 0, either no error or undetectable error pattern
Each non-zero syndrome corresponds to unique error pattern (for single-error-correcting)
Decoding process: calculate syndrome, look up error pattern, subtract error from received vector
For (n,k) code, syndrome has r bits (r = n-k)
Can correct up to t errors if code distance permits
Complexity: requires syndrome lookup table of size 2^r
